body:
It's a bit more complicated.

We don't promise that frpc can always send msg.CloseProxy to frps before exiting. Instead, you should inject logic here https://github.com/fatedier/frp/blob/dev/server/control.go#L375 when we really close and release proxy.

And we should not block on server plugin calls for `CloseProxy` message.  It's better to send notify requests asynchronously. We don't need to wait for plugin's response. It's just a notify message.

For some special cases, such as network error between frpc and frps, it has risk to send all `CloseProxy` messages to plugins. It will create too many connections if there are lots of frpcs and proxies.
author: fatedier

body:
This now works based on my local tests and e2e tests.

> For some special cases, such as network error between frpc and frps, it has risk to send all CloseProxy messages to plugins. It will create too many connections if there are lots of frpcs and proxies.

I think that it's fine, and the plugin itself should handle this situation and aggregate (or delay) the notifications so that it does not trigger rate limits or annoy the user, we shouldn't handle this on our own.
author: kmod-midori

body:
> I think that it's fine, and the plugin itself should handle this situation and aggregate (or delay) the notifications so that it does not trigger rate limits or annoy the user, we shouldn't handle this on our own.

Since we use HTTP/1.1，frps will create new connections for each new concurrent request. It will consume many fds and there are also local ports limit (maybe 30000 - 60000) to the same plugin port.

It's nothing about plugin. frps will get network error in that scenario.
author: fatedier

body:
What about one `CloseProxy` event per `frpc` in `stoper`? We can have a `ProxyNames []string` field for this. Aggregating the shutdown of multiple `frpc`s seems difficult.

Since it is still possible for a client to close a single proxy by sending `CloseProxy` messages, we need to handle this case as well, put a single name in that array.

body:
需求和理由
1、暴露服务到公网的需求：教会所有rdp/ssh等用户去使用stcp，并不现实。比如你的老板，导师，以及很少使用这些功能的朋友，同事。因此总会暴露部分服务到公网端口上，并承受相关风险。
2、服务端防护？Windows端的暴力破解防护，不知道在哪，日志发现，每隔2-3分钟就会出现一次rdp登录错误，持续几个小时，window防火墙没有丝毫反应。sshd端防护的话，需要proxy_protocol外加 mmproxy，配置繁杂。
3、frps服务器端，利用fail2ban 检测那些重复性的“持续时间很短”的frp连接，屏蔽容易很多，而且屏蔽掉那些暴力破解流量后，frps的资源占用也会少很多。

I misunderstood the previous question a little bit. It's more difficult to support on server side.

Your change can't take effect for all proxy types, udp & http? They don't call `HandleUserTCPConnection` function.

And it should not take effect both on client and server side. It's a bit of a waste of resources.

I need to consider more about this feature later.
author: fatedier

body:
> I misunderstood the previous question a little bit. It's more difficult to support on server side.
> 
> Your change can't take effect for all proxy types, udp & http? They don't call `HandleUserTCPConnection` function.
> 
> And it should not take effect both on client and server side. It's a bit of a waste of resources.
> 
> I need to consider more about this feature later.

Thank you for the reply @fatedier, I see what you mean for other proxy types, I can investigate that and update the PR.

As for client and server setting this and wasting resources, is that the same for encryption and compression?  If the client controls the server, then they know whether or not it will be set and can avoid wasting their own resources.  If they do not control the server, then there is no way of knowing for sure.

Thinking about the possibilities if the client _does not_ control the server, for example:
* If the server sets a 10MB limit, but a client sets a 5MB limit, then 5MB will be the limit - client is able to additionally control the lower limit, if necessary
* If the server sets a 10MB limit, but a client sets a 20MB limit, then 10MB will be the limit - server is able to enforce the upper limit

There would be some wasted resources in the above scenarios, but there is also some additional functionality and control that can be achieved by each separate party (client and server).

Let me know if I can help out in any way.

body:
@blizard863 Do you have time to help fix and test this issue? In the `ctl.authSetter.SetPing` in the client, if it fails, it should continue to the next iteration instead of exiting directly. This will cause the entire message processing loop to exit.

In addition, OIDC and token are different here. The time required here is unpredictable, so it is necessary to start a new goroutine to handle it instead of synchronous processing.

author: NanamiNakano

body:
I have some simple ideas about refactoring in configuration management, but they are not perfect. There will likely be significant changes in this area in the future. Without clear specifications, I am currently not inclined to add any extra functionality.

Currently, after you have modified the configuration file, you can use the `frpc reload` command to reload the configuration file, or use the functionality provided on the web page to modify the configuration and reload. Compared to introducing the inotify API, I believe these are already sufficient.

You can also use another tool to watch for file updates and then call the reload interface of frpc. This combination may also be more in line with the philosophy of Unix systems.

body:
Thanks for your contribution.

However, for such functionality, we need to be more cautious in our design and thinking. We usually do not tend to directly submit code, but rather submit proposals and specific implementation paths in the issue, discuss and reach a consensus before attempting to code, in order to avoid wasting valuable time due to inconsistencies with project planning.

There has been no progress on this issue for various reasons. One reason is that I am not clear on how useful this functionality is in the current network environment. This capability may require testing and long-term maintenance in various network environments, which would increase the project's maintenance costs. We should only consider it when the benefits are clearly greater than the added costs. Additionally, during the transition period of major version refactoring, we hope to minimize the complexity brought by new features.
author: fatedier

body:
Thank you very much for your prompt and timely response. I understand and agree with your concerns.

With the development of technologies such as P2P, initiating UPnP requests and asking routers to cooperate is a common technique in scenarios requiring public network mapping. Investigation and testing in this context, as outlined in #1823, demonstrate that even within ISP-level NAT networks, some providers offer support for related technologies.

In IPv6 environments, even though each endpoint can acquire an IPv6 address, most routers currently block all inbound requests from the internet for security reasons. Therefore, it's still necessary to configure the opening of corresponding ports. The use of UPnP in these scenarios retains significant value.

Moreover, some tools also offer similar functionalities, like ZeroTier (https://www.zerotier.com/).

The current implementation is a straightforward extension of the STUN implementation for xTCP.

After acquiring a public IP address, a callback is invoked. If permitted by the configuration, the UPnP feature is used to direct the router to transfer the externally exposed port to an open UDP port, thereby "creating" an EasyNAT in this environment. The subsequent process fully reuses the STUN procedure.

I haven't closely examined the current implementation, but my assumption is that either the visitor or the proxy needs to be reachable by the other party for a direct connection to be established without the need for server relay. Therefore, I have implemented related configuration code on both the visitor and the proxy sides.

If there's any misunderstanding on my part, please kindly correct me.

I suggest creating a new issue to track this proposal, and it may be necessary to pay attention to the following points:

1. The implementation methods and configuration options in other similar projects, such as the ZeroTier you mentioned above. We should try to draw on the solutions of mature projects as much as possible.
2. Seek some early users to help test and verify in which scenarios the solution can indeed solve the problem.
3. What side effects might there be, and how to avoid them.
